title: Why LoRA Stabilizes Training
concept: By updating only a tiny fraction of parameters, LoRA prevents the model from forgetting its general world knowledge. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA.'

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of using LoRA during model training?

options.text: To train models stably without forgetting general knowledge
To make the model generate random text faster
To completely replace neural networks with spreadsheets

exercises.explain: By modifying only a tiny fraction of parameters, LoRA keeps training stable and prevents the model from forgetting what it already knows.

cards.title: Avoiding the Brain Wipe
cards.body: When we update all parameters of a giant AI model on a new task, it often suffers from 'catastrophic forgetting'—it learns the new task but completely breaks its general reasoning skills. How do we keep the AI stable?

cards.title: What is LoRA Stabilization?
cards.body: LoRA keeps the massive, original brain of the AI frozen (unchanged). It only trains a tiny set of extra parameters. Because the core foundation doesn't change, the model won't 'forget' how to write, think, or speak while learning a new specific skill.

cards.title: The Sticky Note Encyclopedia
cards.body: Imagine you are editing a massive, bestselling encyclopedia. Instead of rewriting all 1,000 pages (which might introduce typos and ruin the original stories), you simply stick a few sticky notes with local updates on the margins. The original encyclopedia remains perfectly intact, but you still get the custom info you need!

cards.title: LoRA in Action
cards.body: When developers fine-tune a medical AI using LoRA, they freeze the base model. The AI retains its general conversational skills and grammar. The small LoRA parameters successfully learn specialized medical terms without messing up the model's basic ability to speak English.

cards.title: Myth: Fewer Parameters Mean Worse Results
cards.body: Some think that because LoRA only updates a tiny fraction of parameters, the model won't learn the new task deeply. In reality, these small updates are highly targeted and often achieve the same or better performance than updating the entire model, without the instability!

cards.title: Comparing Training Methods
cards.body: Let's compare what happens to the model's knowledge during training under both approaches:

right.text: Keeps base weights frozen. Adds small side parameters. Safe, stable, and fast.

left.text: Updates every weight. Easy to overwrite base knowledge and break general reasoning.

exercises.prompt: Why does traditional full fine-tuning often cause a model to lose its general world knowledge?

options.text: It overwrites the original parameters that stored the general knowledge
It shrinks the model size to make it run faster on phones
It deletes the dataset after the training completes
It only allows the model to read images instead of text

exercises.explain: Updating every single parameter during training can overwrite previous knowledge, causing the AI to forget its general language capabilities.

exercises.prompt: True or False: LoRA helps prevent catastrophic forgetting by keeping the base model weights frozen.
exercises.explain: LoRA keeps the original weights frozen, meaning the fundamental knowledge is never overwritten or damaged.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete how LoRA protects a model's memory:
exercises.sentence: By keeping the original model weights frozen, LoRA prevents catastrophic _blank_ during fine-tuning.
exercises.explain: When we freeze the main weights, we prevent the model from overwriting its core knowledge, avoiding catastrophic forgetting.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes how LoRA stabilizes training?

options.text: It freezes the original model parameters and trains a tiny set of helper weights.
It continuously randomizes all parameter weights to keep the model guessing.
It forces the user to manually rewrite the attention layers before training starts.

exercises.explain: The first statement is correct because LoRA specifically freezes the main base model weights to keep training stable. The others describe traditional full training or random deletions.

exercises.prompt: How exactly does LoRA stabilize training and prevent a model from losing its general world knowledge?

options.text: It changes the model's architecture by deleting random layers
It freezes the original weights and only updates a tiny, separate set of adapter parameters
It forces the model to memorize only the new training samples and ignore old data
It increases the size of the original weight matrices so they can hold more info

exercises.explain: By freezing the original weights, LoRA ensures that the fundamental language abilities and factual knowledge of the model are never overwritten, avoiding catastrophic forgetting while using minimal GPU memory. Next up: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA.
